chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number:	VIST 487 / CSCE 443
Course Title:	Game Development
Section:	501
Time:	M/W 1:50 pm - 3:30 pm
Location:	ARCC 109A
Credit Hours:	3 (2:2)

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor:	Stewart Ziff
Office:	2RPK (1700 Research Pkwy) room#: TBD
Phone:	n/a
E-Mail:	ziff@tamu.edu
Office Hours:	By appointment, Wed 9am-1pm, Fri 10am-5pm, Zoom or in person.

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Aesthetic and technical aspects of computer game development, including game mechanics, story
development, content creation and game programming; includes game design, interface design, 3D
modeling and animation, graphics algorithms, shader programming and artificial intelligence; group
project includes the design and development of a game from start to finish.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in VIST 386 or CSCE 441, or approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: There is no Special Course Designation for this course.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

*   Demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of the digital game development and production
    pipeline, from initial ideation to public release.
*   Develop and present a detailed Game Design Document (GDD) outlining the design of a digital
    game.
*   Communicate the core design concepts of their game effectively to peers, instructors, and
    industry professionals.
*   Create and manage a production schedule to ensure timely achievement of development
    milestones.
*   Contribute to various roles within the digital game development process.
*   Apply industry-standard practices and methodologies in the development of digital games.
*   Focus on critical development elements that support the game's core design, including
    prototypes, vertical slices, demos, and development builds.
*   Deliver a polished, portfolio-quality or professional-grade final build of their team's game,
    suitable for public distribution.
*   Analyze and showcase their game's gameplay elements in a compelling video trailer.
*   Establish a public presence for their games via release and distribution on digital platforms and
    submitting it to events, festivals, competitions, and conferences.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
There is no required textbook for the course, however the following are recommended texts:

*   "Game Design Workshop: A Playcentric Approach to Creating Innovative Games" by Tracy
    Fullerton.
*   "The Art of Game Design: A Book of Lenses" by Jesse Schell
*   "Rules of Play: Game Design Fundamentals" by Katie Salen and Eric Zimmerman

chunks.title: Assignment Structure
chunks.content:
This course is organized around a single semester-long group game project, developed in production
teams of 5-6 students. While the project is structured through required milestone checkpoints (GDD,
workflow/pipeline plan, prototype, vertical slice, beta, and final), there is no formal grading assigned to
individual milestones. Instead, checkpoints function as production deadlines for feedback,
accountability, and scope control. Final grading is determined through an end-of-semester evaluation of
(1) the completed group game and (2) each student's individual contribution to the group's production
process. At Spring Break (mid-semester) each team will receive a Work-in-Progress report summarizing
status, risks, and recommended next steps; this report is feedback-only and not a graded evaluation.

chunks.title: Assessment Framework
chunks.content:
Final evaluation uses a standardized three-category rubric:

1.  Aesthetics (expression, narrativity & storyline, originality) - group score
2.  Technical (functionality, systems, pipeline, performance) - group score
3.  Individual Contribution / Personal Involvement (participation and production labor) - individual
    score

Scoring Logic
Each category is evaluated on a 1-3 point scale using the same structural logic across criteria:
3 = Strong / Exceeds Expectations · 2 = Competent / Meets Expectations · 1 = Developing / Below
Expectations
Group categories (Expression + Technical) are evaluated for the project as a whole. The Individual
Contribution category is scored separately for each student.

chunks.title: General Project Grading Rubric (Standardized Template)
chunks.content:
Scoring Range (Group Project): 2 (minimum) - 6 (maximum total points)
The group project grade is determined by combining the two group categories below (Aesthetics +
Technical). Each category receives a 1-3 score.

I. Aesthetics (1–3 points)
Evaluates the coherence and strength of the game's aesthetic identity, narrative/world-building
approach (where applicable), player-facing experience, and originality of concept and execution.
3 points: The game demonstrates a distinctive, cohesive expressive vision. Aesthetic choices
(visual/audio/style), narrative or thematic elements, and overall player experience reinforce one
another with clarity and intention. The project shows creative risk-taking and originality, and the
expressive layer strengthens engagement and meaning.
2 points: The game is conceptually clear and competently presented. Aesthetic and narrative elements
generally support the experience, though cohesion, distinctiveness, or refinement may be uneven.
Originality is present but not fully developed.
1 point: Expressive direction is unclear or inconsistent. Aesthetic choices and narrative framing (if used)
do not effectively support the intended experience, and the game lacks cohesion, clarity, or an
identifiable creative voice.

II. Technical (1–3 points)
Assesses the functional integrity and technical craft of the game, including systems implementation,
stability, performance, and production discipline (workflow, integration, and build reliability).
3 points: The game is stable and reliably playable, with strong technical execution across core systems.
Mechanics function as intended, performance is well managed, and pipeline practices (version control,
integration, task discipline, documentation) support professional-level delivery with minimal major
flaws.
2 points: The game is playable and technically coherent, with minor bugs or roughness that do not
significantly block play. Core systems work as expected, and production workflow is functional, though
not consistently refined or robust.
1 point: Technical shortcomings significantly impact playability, clarity, or stability. Core systems are
incomplete or unreliable, performance issues interfere with play, and/or the workflow/pipeline is
disorganized in ways that hinder production.

chunks.title: Individual Contribution Rubric (Per-Student Category)
chunks.content:
Scoring Range (Individual): 1 (minimum) – 3 (maximum total points)
This category is scored separately for each student and measures the consistency and impact of their
involvement in the group's production process. The score reflects participation, reliability, tangible work
output, collaboration, and ownership of responsibilities.

III. Individual Contribution / Personal Involvement (1–3 points)
3 points: Consistently reliable, highly engaged contributor. Produces substantive, high-quality work that
integrates into the project and improves the overall outcome. Communicates proactively, supports team
workflow, and demonstrates ownership and initiative in solving problems and advancing milestones.
2 points: Regularly engaged and dependable contributor. Completes assigned responsibilities with
usable, competent output and participates constructively in collaboration and decision-making.
Communication is generally clear and timely.
1 point: Inconsistent or minimal involvement. Missed responsibilities, limited output, or weak follow-
through places added burden on teammates. Communication and collaboration are insufficient to
support production needs.

chunks.title: Note on Milestones and Final Evaluation
chunks.content:
Milestones are required and used to guide production pacing, ensure progress, and provide feedback,
but they are not individually graded. Final evaluation is based on the end-of-semester review of each
team's completed game (Expression + Technical) and each student's demonstrated production
involvement (Individual Contribution), with the Spring Break Work-in-Progress report serving as a non-
graded midpoint benchmark.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Below is a list of graded activities in the course and their corresponding percentage of the final grade.

Group Project (70%) ... This portion reflects the completed game as a group outcome, evaluated at the
end of the semester using the two-category rubric (Aesthetics + Technical).
Individual Contribution (30%) ... This portion reflects each student's personal involvement in the
production process, evaluated using the Individual Contribution / Personal Involvement rubric.

Aesthetics (Group): 35%
Technical (Group): 35%
Personal Involvement (Individual): 30%
Total: 100%

A final letter grade is determined as follows:

A = 90%-100% B = 80%-89% C = 70%-79% D = 60%-69% F = below 60%

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
*   Deliverables will not be accepted late unless by prior arrangement with the instructor. Failure to
    submit work on the due date will result in a penalty of 5 percentage points per day for the
    deliverable, up to 5 days. Later than that will result in a zero.
*   Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late
    work and is exempted from the late work policy. (See Student Rule 7.)
*   Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when
    attendance contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other
    reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.
*   Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including
    definitions, and related documentation and timelines.
*   Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of
    more than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed
    upon by the student and instructor” (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.1).
*   "The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up
    work missed because of an unexcused absence" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2).
*   Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and
    Student Conduct Code. (See Student Rule 24.)

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Weeks 1–4 Concept, Scope, and Production Foundations
Primary Course Learning Objective Focus: CLO 1 (Analyze and Plan) & CLO 2 (Demonstrate and Apply)
Focus: Teams form, establish a shared production language, and move quickly from idea to executable
plan. The emphasis is on scope discipline and clarity: defining the core narrative, the primary player loop,
and the minimum set of mechanics required for a playable experience. Students produce a Game Design
Document (end of Week 2) and then translate that vision into an implementable pipeline: a workflow
chart, role delineation, and production schedule (end of Week 3). Studio time alternates between
targeted workshops (documentation, version control, task tracking, core engine setup) and open lab
where teams begin greyboxing and prototyping with an early emphasis on “first playable” thinking.
Key Skills Developed: Team roles and collaboration norms; scoping and risk assessment; GDD writing;
workflow mapping and sprint planning; source control/build discipline; early prototyping and
greyboxing.

Weeks 5-8 Prototype to Vertical Slice
Primary Course Learning Objective Focus: CLO 2 (Demonstrate and Apply) & CLO 3 (Design and Visualize)
Focus: Production accelerates through iterative builds, with required check-ins every second week to
surface blockers and keep scope realistic. Teams hit a prototype milestone (end of Week 5) that proves
the core loop works, then push toward a vertical slice milestone (end of Week 8) ... a short,
representative segment delivered at a defined quality bar. Vertical slice work is guided by explicit
vertical slice targets (scope, feature set, content readiness, polish, performance, a complete start-to-
finish player flow within the vertical slice (menu → play → success/fail → return/loop). Workshops focus
on systems integration (gameplay states, interactions, UI foundations), content pipelines (assets,
animation, integration), and applied graphics/shader and Al fundamentals as needed by each project.
Key Skills Developed: Core mechanics implementation; UI/UX foundations; content integration
pipelines; applied graphics/materials and lighting; basic game Al patterns; sprint reviews and iteration;
defining and meeting vertical slice targets.

Weeks 9–12 Playtesting, Iteration, and Beta Readiness
Primary Course Learning Objective Focus: CLO 3 (Design and Visualize) & CLO 4 (Integrate and Operate)
Focus: The course shifts from "making it work” to “making it playable for others." Teams design and
execute a structured playtesting period, collect actionable feedback, and triage fixes by severity and
impact. Systems are expanded and stabilized through production sprints, culminating in a Beta / feature-
complete milestone (end of Week 12): the game is playable start-to-finish, major content is in place, and
the team maintains a known-bugs list plus a clear polish plan. Midway through the semester (around
Spring Break), each group receives a Work-in-Progress report summarizing status, risks, and
recommended priorities-this is feedback-only and supports course pacing, not a graded milestone.
Key Skills Developed: Playtest planning and facilitation; feedback synthesis and bug triage; stability and
performance tuning; onboarding/tutorial clarity; feature lock discipline; build management and QA
workflows; iterative design grounded in player evidence.

Weeks 13-15 Polish, Release Candidate, and Final Showcase
Primary Course Learning Objective Focus: CLO 4 (Integrate and Operate) & CLO 5 (Evaluate and Reflect)
Focus: Teams enter a polish-and-release mindset: refining game feel, balancing, improving clarity,
optimizing performance, and hardening the build into a release candidate. Studio time is predominantly
open lab, with targeted critique moments to ensure teams are truly "shipping" rather than endlessly
expanding. The semester concludes with final presentations in Week 15, requiring a functional working
game delivered on schedule, accompanied by a concise account of the team's workflow, engine
rationale, playtesting results, and key design decisions. A postmortem-style reflection emphasizes what
the production revealed-about scope, collaboration, technical choices, and aesthetic intent.
Key Skills Developed: Polish and tuning; performance optimization and packaging; final
UX/accessibility/readability pass; presentation and demonstration skills; production postmortem and
reflective evaluation; professional communication of process and outcomes.

chunks.title: Weekly Schedule
chunks.content:
| Week | Lecture | Lab | Assignments / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Module 1 Concept & Preproduction Weeks 1-4 | | | |
| 1A Jan 12, 2026 | Course overview; team formation; engine options and scope | Tooling setup (engine, repo, task board); ideation kickoff | Form teams; begin concept exploration |
| 1B Jan 14, 2026 | GDD structure: mechanics, narrative, UX, technical requirements | Paper prototype + rapid play; define risks and scope | Concept pitch draft + initial feature list |
| 2A Jan 19, 2026 | MLK Day NO CLASS | | GDD draft for review |
| 2B Jan 21, 2026 | Game Pitch Presentation | Game Pitch Presentation | |
| 3A Jan 26, 2026 | Pipeline planning: source control, task tracking, build targets. Workflow / roles. | Open lab: Create workflow chart (assets/code), naming conventions, repo structure. lock responsibilities | Milestone 1: GDD v1 due |
| 3B Jan 28, 2026 | GDD presentations + critique and scope calibration | GDD presentations | Milestone 2: workflow chart + roles + production schedule due |
| 4A Feb 02, 2026 | Sprint planning: first playable path and production priorities | Implement graybox; input, camera, core interaction baseline | Update schedule and risk list |
| 4B Feb 04, 2026 | WIP check-in #1: progress demo + blockers + scope adjustments | Open lab: address feedback; stabilize build | |
| Module 2 Prototype to Vertical Slice Weeks 5-8 | | | |
| 5A Feb 09, 2026 | Core systems workshop (team-specific mechanics and architecture) | Integrate mechanics; begin Ul baseline; establish content pipeline | Prototype readiness checklist |
| 5B Feb 11, 2026 | Prototype milestone review and critique | Stability pass + build packaging | Milestone 3: initial playable prototype / graybox due |
| 6A Feb 16, 2026 | UI/UX patterns: onboarding, feedback, readability; persistence basics | Implement onboarding + UI flows; save/load if applicable | Usability pass; document known UX issues |
| 6B Feb 18, 2026 | WIP check-in #2: updated build; vertical slice targets confirmed | Open lab: lock vertical slice scope and quality bar | Vertical slice plan finalized |
| 7A Feb 23, 2026 | Graphics pipeline: materials/shaders, lighting, performance budgets | Art direction pass; materials/lighting/VFX iteration | Update art direction + performance targets |
| 7B Feb 25, 2026 | Game Al foundations: state/behavior trees, navigation, encounters | Implement Al behaviors; tune difficulty and pacing | Vertical slice integration plan |
| 8A March 02, 2026 | Vertical slice assembly: start-to-finish slice polish | Internal QA; build stability; content integration | Prepare milestone demo + build |
| 8B March 04, 2026 | Vertical slice presentations + critique (also WIP check-in #3) | Backlog triage; revise sprint plan post-review | Milestone 4: vertical slice build + known-issues list due |
| SPRING BREAK March 09-13, 2026 | | | Mid-semester Work-in-Progress (WIP) report issued to each group (not graded). |
| Module 3 Playtesting, Iteration, Beta Weeks 9-12 | | | |
| 9A March 16, 2026 | Playtesting methods: hypotheses, scripts, observation, bug taxonomy | Draft playtest plan + feedback instruments | Playtest plan due |
| 9B March 18, 2026 | Test build preparation: packaging, distribution, instrumentation (optional) | Finalize testable build; assign playtest roles | Playtest-ready build |
| 10A March 23, 2026 | In-class playtesting session (cross-team) | Collect observations, bugs, and friction points; debrief | Playtest findings summary |
| 10B March 25, 2026 | WIP check-in #4: report findings; triage and iteration priorities | Open lab: implement highest-impact fixes | Prioritized fix list + sprint goals |
| 11A March 30, 2026 | Debugging and profiling; optimization strategy | Performance pass; reduce technical debt | Optimization log / changelog update |
| 11B April 01, 2026 | Iteration sprint: onboarding, balance, clarity, pacing | Integrate revisions; stabilize build | Update documentation and task board |
| 12A April 06, 2026 | Feature lock prep; accessibility/readability pass | Integrate content; QA checklist; prepare beta | Beta readiness checklist |
| 12B April 08, 2026 | Beta / feature-complete presentations (also WIP check-in #5) | Polish roadmap; final risk review | Milestone 5: beta build + polish plan due |
| Module 4 Polish & Release Weeks 13-15 | | | |
| 13A April 13, 2026 | Polish: game feel, balance, feedback loops, audio/VFX cues | Polish sprint: tune, refine, and remove friction | Plan targeted peer test |
| 13B April 15, 2026 | Targeted peer test + polish implementation | Fix issues; finalize content; tighten performance | Release candidate checklist |
| 14A April 20, 2026 | Release candidate workflow: packaging, build management, QA | Final QA; compliance with submission requirements | Internal RC build |
| 14B April 22, 2026 | Dress rehearsal / WIP check-in #6: near-final demo + presentation plan | Finalize presentation materials and documentation | Final submission checklist |
| 15A April 27, 2026 | Final showcase: presentations + live demo/play | Peer play + feedback collection | Final build + documentation due |
| 15B April 29, 2026 | READING DAY No Class. | | |

chunks.title: Canvas (LMS)
chunks.content:
Information on Canvas is for the sole use of Texas A&M University students enrolled in
the class. This online repository contains materials (images, articles, etc.) copyrighted by others that
have been included for the purpose of instruction only, under the "fair use" provision of the U.S.
copyright laws. Copying these materials to any other site or use of these materials for any other purpose
is prohibited. Please log in at https://canvas.tamu.edu/

chunks.title: Technology Support
chunks.content:
if you encounter technical difficulties, please contact ITS/Helpdesk for any
technical support with your LMS, computer, and software. Contact the Help Desk directly at
979-845-8300. Pipeline technical support e.g. (UV layout is not correct, importing assets in Unreal, etc.)
you should contact the instructor and/or the TA by email.

chunks.title: Learning Resources
chunks.content:
Please seek additional learning resources such as the TAMU University Writing
Center or related LinkedIn Learning modules appropriate for the course topics.

chunks.title: LinkedIn Learning
chunks.content:
Sign in by using your TAMU email
https://www.linkedin.com/learning?trk=homepage-learning_nav-header-logo

The Study Hub website also lists many on-campus learning resources to support you in achieving
academic excellence.

chunks.title: Texas A&M College Station
chunks.content:
Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services (979.458.4584) 24-hour
emergency help is also available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (988) or at 988lifeline.org.

chunks.title: College and Department Policies
chunks.content:
Please respect your facilities: studio space, photo lab, shop, labs, etc.

The use of spray paint, spray adhesive or other surface-altering materials is not permitted in the
Langford Complex, except in designated zones (we do have spray booth facilities located on our
complex). Students who violate this rule will be liable for the expenses associated with repairing
damaged building finishes and surfaces.

Throughout the semester and at the end of the semester, your area must be clean of all trash. You are
responsible for cleaning your studio, desk, and locker at the end of each semester.

No power tools may be used in the design studio. No dust or odor-producing processes may be
conducted in the studio. No wet casting processes may be conducted in the studio. The college shop and
spray booth facilities must be used for the above-mentioned processes. Professional behavior and
conduct are expected of each student.

All studio desks must be covered. In addition, students must have at minimum an 18" x 24" cutting mat
at their desk.

chunks.title: Studio Policy (Required of all Studios)
chunks.content:
All students, faculty, administration, and staff of the School of Performance, Visualization & Fine Arts at
Texas A&M University are dedicated to the principle that the Design Studio is the central component of
an effective education. They are equally dedicated to the belief that students and faculty must lead
balanced lives and use time wisely, including time outside the design studio, to gain from all aspects of a
university education and world experiences. They also believe that design is the integration of many
parts, that process is as important as product, and that the act of design and of professional practice is
inherently interdisciplinary, requiring active and respectful collaboration with others.

Students and faculty in every design studio will embody the fundamental values of optimism, respect,
sharing, engagement, and innovation. Every design studio will therefore encourage the rigorous
exploration of ideas, diverse viewpoints, and the integration of all aspects of visualization (practical,
theoretical, scientific, spiritual, and artistic), by providing a safe and supportive environment for
thoughtful innovation. Every design studio will increase skills in professional communication, through
drawing, modeling, writing, and speaking.

chunks.title: Artificial Intelligence Statements
chunks.content:
In this course, you are permitted to thoughtfully explore and experiment with A.I. tools as part of your
creative and academic process. These tools are valuable for brainstorming, drafting, visualizing,
problem-solving, and other aspects of your work. Whenever you use A.I. to support your assignments or
projects, you must clearly indicate which tools were used and describe how they contributed to your
process. This includes citing the A.I. application and briefly explaining the nature of its role (for example:
idea generation, editing, drafting, visualization, coding assistance, etc.). The expectation is that all
submitted work reflects your own critical engagement, creativity, and understanding. A.I. may assist
you, but it should not replace your voice, judgment, or learning. If you are uncertain about the
appropriate use of A.I. in a particular assignment, please check with your instructor before submission.
